(86) with the parameters χ0 = 1, a = 0.15]. The force density diverges in the macroscopic limit (N → ∞). A. Vacuum forces According to Hamilton’s equations, the force on each scat- terer is given by the negative derivative of the Hamiltonian with respect to the position xi. For the Hamiltonian we take the zero–point energy of the electromagnetic field expressed as the integral of all vacuum energies E/2 times the density of states D(E). Here E represents the energy E = ℏω cor- responding to the frequency ω. The density of states is given by the Friedel–Krein–Lloyd formula51–54 in terms of the scat- tering phase φ as D(E) = (2π)−1∂Eφ. We integrate by parts and obtain for the van der Waals force Fi = 1 4π Z ∞ 0 ∂iφ dE , E = ℏω . (3) The scattering phase is the sum of all the phases of the eigen- values of the classical scattering matrix S. Since det S is the3 product of all those eigenvalues we may write: φ = −i ln det S . (4) With Eqs. (3) and (4) we have expressed the quantum force on each scatterer in terms of classical quantities. Quantum mechanics enters though E = ℏω and the fact that the zero– point energy is E/2. To proceed, we note that the scattering matrix is unitary, and that all unitary matrices can be expressed via the Cayley transform as S = 1 − iK 1 + iK with Hermitian K. (5) We extend S(E) to negative energies corresponding to neg- ative frequencies. As the classical electromagnetic field is real, the negative–frequency component is the complex conju- gate of the positive frequency component and runs backwards in time. Consequently, S(−E) must be the inverse of S(E). From this follows that K(−E) = −K(E) . (6) We thus obtain Fi = i 4π Z +∞ 0 − Z 0 −∞  ∂i det(1 + iK) det(1 + iK) dE (7) and by deforming the integration contours on the complex plane (Fig. 3) to the positive imaginary axis, and expressing them as an integral over imaginary wave numbers κ with ω = icκ (8) and c being the speed of light in vacuum, we get a rapidly converging formula for the force: Fi = − ℏc 2π Z ∞ 0 ∂i det(1 + iK) det(1 + iK) dκ . (9) We are going to show (Sec. IIC) thatdet(1 + iK) is real on the imaginary axis. Note that we have not performed any renor- malization to calculate the force of the quantum vacuum on the point scatterers. ω icκ FIG. 3: Contour deformation. We represent the integrals R +∞ 0 and − R 0 −∞ over ω [Eq. (7)] by contours (shown as arrows) running to the right or the left, respectively, and move both contours to the positive imaginary axis (ω = icκ) where they coincide, which gives Eq. (9). B. Transfer matrix The only inconvenient feature of formula (9) is the ap- pearance of the scattering matrix (via K). However, for one–dimensional propagation we may expressdet(1 + iK) in terms of the transfer matrix T. The transfer matrix is much easier to calculate than the scattering matrix, because the total transfer matrix of the chain of scatterers is simply the product of the individual transfer matrices55. Consider real frequencies first. A right–moving wave eikx on the left of the structure is transferred to the superposition α eikx + β e−ikx with complex α and β on the right (not to be confused with the polarizabilities αi) while a left–moving wave e−ikx on the left gets transferred to β∗ eikx + α∗ e−ikx on the right. The transfer matrix has thus the structure55 T =  α β ∗ β α ∗  (10) with det T = |α|2 − |β|2 > 0 as the outgoing waves must not exceed the ingoing waves. The scattering matrix S describes how ingoing waves are turned into outgoing waves. The in- going waves enter as eikx on the left and e−ikx on the right, while the outgoing waves emerge ase−ikx on the left and eikx on the right. One verifies that the coefficients of the outgoing and ingoing waves are related by the matrix S = 1 α∗ p |α|2 − |β|2 β∗ −β p |α|2 − |β|2  . (11) We see from Eq. (11) that det S = α/α∗ and infer from Eq. (10) and det S = det( 1 + iK)∗/ det(1 + iK) the rela- tion det(1 + iK) = T22 . (12) This relation, once established on the real axis of E, is ex- tended by complex continuation to the imaginary axis (Fig. 3) where we need it. Having made the connection between the transfer matrix and the force, we use the product rule of transfer matrices to express T in terms of the individual transfer matrices. Be- tween the scatterers at positions xi the field propagates freely (Fig. 1). The transfer matrices of free propagation are given by Pi =  e−κδi 0 0 eκδi  with δi = xi+1 − xi (13) for purely imaginary wavenumbers where waves decay expo- nentially in propagation direction. For the sake of calculating the force, we keep the distance δi variable, as we need to dif- ferentiate with respect to the coordinates. The beginning and the end of the propagation region outside of the scatterers we put at the fixed points x0 and xN+1. The precise values of these points will not influence the force. It is wise to break the total transfer matrix into two parts Aj and Bj describing the transfer from x0 to xj−1 and from xj to xN+1, respectively.4 We denote the transfer matrices of the scatterers by Ri and obtain for the partial transfer matrices the recurrence relations Aj+1 = Pj Rj Aj and Bj−1 = Bj Rj Pj−1 (14) with the initial values A1 = P0 and BN = PN . (15) With the help of the A and B matrices we get for the total transfer matrix: T = Bj Rj Aj . (16) As ∂j = ∂δj−1 − ∂δj we obtain from Eq. (13) for the deriva- tives of the transfer matrix ∂jT = κBj [σz, Rj] Aj (17) written in terms of the commutator with the Pauli matrixσz = diag(1, −1). We have arrived at expressions for the force that are conve- nient for numerical calculations (Appendix A) and, as we shall see in Sec. III, for deducing the force density in the macro- scopic limit. C. Point scatterers It remains to specify the transfer matrices Ri of the point scatterers (Fig. 1). We describe a point scatterer at position xi as a delta–function susceptibility in the Helmholtz equation of the electromagnetic field. We only consider one polariza- tion component of the field and denote it by ψ (for the other polarization we would get the same). For purely imaginary wavenumbers we thus have ∂2 xψ = κ2 [1 + αiδ(x − xi)] ψ . (18) The field to the left of xi we write as the superposition a−e−κx + a+eκx while the field on the right is given by the (different) superposition b−e−κx + b+eκx. To satisfy Eq. (18) the field ψ must be continuous at xi while the derivative ψ′ is discontinuous and changes by κ2αiψ(xi). From this we ob- tain the connection between the coefficients, i.e. the transfer matrix: Ri = 1 − καi 2 (σz + iσy) (19) expressed here in terms of the Pauli matrices σx =  0 1 1 0  , σ y =  0 −i i 0  , σ z =  1 0 0 −1  (20) for mathematical convenience. Note that the R matrices are real, and so are the P matrices (13) describing the free propa- gation between the scatterers. Therefore the total transfer ma- trix and its derivatives are real, too, and so is det(1 + iK) as we have stated in Sec. IIA. From the commutation relations of the Pauli matrices (20) we get the relation[σz, Ri] = −καiσx and hence from Eq. (17): ∂jT = −κ2αjBjσxAj . (21) This completes the calculation of the van der Waals force on a chain of point particles using scattering theory and the transfer matrix. III. MACROSCOPIC LIMIT In the macroscopic limit we assume that the inter–particle distance δ goes to zero with finite ratio (1) between polariz- ability αi and δ. We translate the recurrence relations (14) into differential equations and express the solution for the force density in terms of the macroscopic Green function. A. Differential equations Take the partial transfer matrices A and B as continuous functions of x evaluated at the points xj of the scatterers. Substituting αi = χδ in Eq. (19) and using the recurrence relations (14) for the A and B matrices we linearize the differ- ences Aj+1 − Aj and Bj − Bj−1 taking also into account the δ–dependence in the propagation matrices (13). We obtain the differential equations ∂xA = −κVA and ∂xB = κBV with V = σz + χ 2 (σz + iσy) (22) in terms of the Pauli matrices (20). We obtain from the initial values (15) in the macroscopic limit (whereP → 1) the initial conditions A(x0) = B(xN+1) = 1 . (23) According to Eqs. (9) and (12) the force is given in terms of the matrix element T22 and its spatial derivative T ′ 22. Since R → 1 for δ → 0 we obtain from Eq. (16) T = BA, and so T22 = B21A12 + B22A22 . (24) From Eq. (21) we get T ′ 22 = −κ2χδ (B21A22 + B22A12) . (25) One verifies that for the relevant matrix elements the solutions of the differential equations (22) have the structure A12 = (1 − κ−1∂x)ψ+ , A 22 = (1 + κ−1∂x)ψ+ B21 = (1 + κ−1∂x)ψ− , B 22 = (−1 + κ−1∂x)ψ− (26) where the ψ± satisfy the Helmholtz equation ∂2 xψ = κ2(1 + χ)ψ . (27) From the initial condition (23) and the structure (26) of the solution follows the asymptotics ψ± ∝ e±κx for x → ∓∞ . (28) Taken all this together we obtain T ′ 22 T22 = −κ2χδ (ψ+ψ−)′ W (29) where W denotes the Wronskian W = ψ+ψ′ − − ψ−ψ′ + . (30)5 Note that the Wronskian (30) is constant in space as a conse- quence of the Helmholtz equation (27). We have seen that the transfer across the scattering struc- ture (and hence the force) is governed by the macroscopic Helmholtz equation (27). This shows that the χ defined in Eq. (1) is indeed the susceptibility and Eq. (2) gives the re- fractive index. In our one–dimensional case34 the susceptibil- ity depends linearly on the density δ−1. In three dimensions, this is normally only the case for dilute fluids. B. Green function In the following we relate the force density in the macro- scopic limit to the Green function. The Green function g(x, x0) describes the field propagation from a monochro- matic point source of unit strength at position x0 to the ob- servation point x, and thus obeys the differential equation (∂2 x − κ2n2)g = δ(x − x0) (31) with the asymptotics of a purely outgoing wave at infinity: g ∝ e±κx for x → ∓∞ . (32) Away from the point of emission (x ̸= x0) the Green function must be a solution of the Helmholtz equation (27). At the emission point g must be continuous; g(x0 + 0, x0) = g(x0 − 0, x0) (33) while the derivative of g needs to jump by 1 in order to gen- erate the delta function on the right–hand side of the Green equation (31): ∂xg(x, x0)|x=x0+0 − ∂xg(x, x0)|x=x0−0 = 1 . (34) The well–known solution of these requirements is the expres- sion g(x, x0) = 1 W ( ψ+(x) ψ−(x0) : x ≤ x0 , ψ+(x0) ψ−(x) : x ≥ x0 . (35) We turn to our calculation of the force in the macroscopic limit, and define the force density as f = F δ . (36) We obtain from Eqs. (9), (12), (29) and (35) the expressions f = ℏc 2π Z ∞ 0 ef dκ , ef = κ2χ ∂xg(x, x) . (37) This is one of the central results of this paper. Similar to the Lifshitz theory18 of the Casimir force we have related the force density to the electromagnetic Green function, but in our case this is the force density inside dielectric media, not the force between dielectric bodies as in Lifshitz’s case18. An- other important difference is the fact that our g is the unrenor- malized Green function — at least for the time being. We shall discuss in Sec. V how renormalization emerges. Figure 2a shows a simple example: the van der Waals forces inside a block of uniform material of length a and refractive index n0 surrounded by vacuum. We see that the force density peaks at the edges of the block, strongly pulling the material in due to van der Waals attraction, whereas near the middle of the block the van der Waals forces from the different parts of material nearly compensate each other. We also see the good agreement between the numerical calculation with the prediction (37) of the macroscopic theory, even for quite a small number of point scatterers (≥ 50). C. Asymptotics In order to understand whether and when formula (37) for the force density converges in the macroscopic limit, we ap- ply the Madelung representation 56 for the fields ψ±. The Helmholtz equation (27) plays the role of the Schr ¨odinger equation in optics, and Madelung has reduced the Schr¨odinger dynamics to fluid mechanics 56. Following Madelung 56 we make the ansatz: ψ± = 1√ 2k exp  ± Z k dx  (38) where the Wronskian (30) is exactly W = −1 . (39) From the Helmholtz equation (27) follows an equation for k that is independent on the ± sign in the exponent, k2 = n2κ2 − 2β , (40) and appears as a generalized dispersion relation with the extra term β = √ k 2 ∂2 x 1√ k = −1 4  k′ k ′ + 1 8  k′ k 2 . (41) For the quantum–mechanical Schr ¨odinger equation, the β– term corresponds to Bohm’s quantum potential57, in optics β describes the scattering in an inhomogeneous medium. Math- ematically, β corresponds to the Schwarzian derivative 58 of the phase. For large frequencies (κ → ∞) relation (40) reduces to the ordinary dispersion relation k2 ∼ n2κ2 of geometrical op- tics. We take the positive sign for k such that the amplitude (2k)−1/2 in the Madelung representation (38) is real. In sub- dominant order we take k2 ∼ [nκ − β/(nκ)]2 and have k ∼ nκ − β0 nκ . (42) Here β0 denotes the dominant contribution to β, i.e. Eq. (41) evaluated for k = nκ. We obtain β0 = −1 4  n′ n ′ + 1 8  n′ n 2 = − n′′ 4n + 3n′2 8n2 (43)6 that does not explicitly depend on κ. The term β0 quantifies the deviation from geometrical optics due to inhomogeneities in the medium, the amplitude of geometrical scattering. In the Madelung representation (38) we get for the Green function of Eq. (35) evaluated atx0 = x the simple expression g(x, x) = −(2k)−1 and hence for the spectral force density of Eq. (37): ef = −κ2(n2 − 1) ∂x 1 2k . (44) For large frequencies (large imaginary wavenumbers κ) we obtain from Eq. (42) the asymptotics: ef ∼ − n2 − 1 2 ∂x  κ n + β0 κn3  . (45) For piece–wise homogeneous media (Figs. 2a and 6a) the derivative in Eq. (45) vanishes, the integral (37) converges, and the force density is finite in the macroscopic limit. At dielectric interfaces (discontinuities of n) the force density does not converge, though. Moreover, any inhomoneity, how- ever small, would produce an infinite force density. The sus- pension of scatterers would instantly and catastrophically col- lapse. This does not happen in reality. So which mechanism — not considered in our model — prevents the catastrophe? IV . HELMHOLTZ FORCE It is wise to take a step back and relate our result (37) to the classical optical forces in dielectrics59. We begin with a brief review on the Helmholtz force in three–dimensional electro- magnetism. Then we turn to our one–dimensional model and identify the quantum forces and stresses there. A. Dielectric deformation Consider three–dimensional electromagnetism in a dielec- tric medium of electric permittivity ε = n2 (and magnetic permeability µ = 1). Forces in the medium cause the dielec- tric to deform. Consider an infinitesimal deformation δx. If we can write the variation of the free energy F as the volume integral δF = − Z f · δx dV (46) then f gives the force density, according to Hamilton’s equa- tions. The part of the free energy modified by the dielectric is the volume integral of the electric energy densityU = 1 2 E ·D with D = ε0εE in SI units. Gauss’ law ∇ · D = ρext gives D in terms of the external electrical charges that are not mod- ified by the deformation of the medium. We thus take D as fixed in the energy density and obtain δU = −U δε/ε. Two terms contribute to δε, the infinitesimal shift of the medium and the change in density ρ, as ε may depend on density: δε = −∇ε · δx + ∂ε ∂ρ δρ (47) Differential geometry60 gives δρ = −ρ∇ · δx. Integrating by parts in the energy produces the structure of Eq. (46) with the force density59 f = − ∇ε ε E · D 2 + ∇pAb (48) and pAb = ∂ε ∂ρ ρ E · D 2ε . (49) The pressure pAb — known as the Abraham pressure 59 — originates from the effect of the density variation on the dielectric: The total force (48) on the medium is called the Helmholtz force 59. For dilute media and in our one– dimensional case, ε = 1 + χ with χ = αρ/ε0 such that we get for the Helmholtz force: f = χ∇ ε0E2 2 = ρ∇ αE2 2 , (50) which is the dipole or gradient force familiar from optical tweezing61–65. B. Quantum force Let us relate our case of van der Waals forces on suspended particles to the Helmholtz force in dielectrics. For this we need to treat the electromagnetic field as a quantum field with the operator bA of the vector potential as fundamental quantity. The electric field strength bE is given by −∂tbA in Coulomb gauge. Consider the correlation functions49 K = ε0c 2ℏ ⟨bA1bA0 + bA0bA1⟩ = ε0c ℏ Re ⟨bA1bA0⟩ , (51) Γ = ε0c 2iℏ ⟨bA1bA0 − bA0bA1⟩ = ε0c ℏ Im ⟨bA1bA0⟩ (52) where the indices refer to the positions xi and times ti. Note that ⟨bA1bA0⟩ is an analytic function of the time difference t = t1 − t0 if the field is in the vacuum state (or thermal) 49. For an analytic function the real and the imaginary part are connected58. Furthermore, Γ corresponds to the difference between the retarded and the advanced Green function 49, the difference between outgoing and ingoing radiation: the dis- sipation. The correlation K of the field fluctuations is thus related to the dissipationΓ, which is a form of the fluctuation– dissipation theorem66. In particular, in terms of our time–independent Green func- tion g and for real frequencies ω we have for the Fourier transform:49 eΓ = g(ω) − g(−ω) 2c . (53) We see thateΓ is an odd function of ω. Consequently, we get for the inverse Fourier transform: Γ(t) = 1 2π Z +∞ −∞ eΓe−iωt dω = − i π Z ∞ 0 eΓ sinωt dω . (54)7 For finding the real part K to the imaginary part Γ we only need to replace − sin ωt by cos ωt, because they represent the imaginary and real part of the analytic e−iωt of time evolution.67 Using Eq. (53) and deforming the integration contours to the positive imaginary axis with wavenumbers κ (Fig. 3) we obtain: K(t) = − 1 π Z ∞ 0 g cosh(cκt) dκ . (55) Strictly speaking, formula (55) is only valid when g falls off more rapidly than cosh(cκt), which is the case outside of the light cone, |x1 − x0| > c|t|, but only there we will need K. From the correlation function K we can calculate the elec- tromagnetic field correlations by differentiation. In particular, we get for the electric fields at equal times, but different posi- tions: ε0 2 ⟨bE1bE0⟩ = ℏ 2c ∂t1 ∂t0 K      t1=t0 = ℏc 2π Z ∞ 0 κ2g(x1, x0) dκ . (56) Compare this with the result (37) for the force density in the macroscopic limit and Eq. (50) for the Helmholtz force: the macroscopic van der Waals force is nothing else but the gradi- ent force61–65. It is a peculiar gradient force, though. The force is not generated by external fields, but rather by the zero–point fluctuations of the field inside the medium. C. Electromagnetic stresses Having established the force density, we are going to relate it now to the electric and magnetic stress tensors59 σE = E ⊗D− E · D 2 1 , σ M = H ⊗B− H · B 2 1 . (57) From Maxwell’s equations and the linear constitutive equa- tions D = ε0εE and H = ε0c2B follows the Abraham identity59 for stationary fields: ∇ · (σE + σM) = − ∇ε ε E · D 2 (58) and from this we get for the Helmholtz force (48): f = ∇ · (σE + σM) + ∇pAb . (59) The force density is thus the divergence of the total electro- magnetic stress plus the gradient of the Abraham pressure. So far we considered the classical stress, but similarly to Sec. IVB we can identify the quantum electromagnetic stresses as well. These stresses are the vacuum expectation values of expressions (57) for the field operators, symmetrized as in definition (51) of the correlation function K. From bE = −∂tbA and bB = ∂xbA (in one dimension) we obtain: eσE = −n2κ2g(x, x) , eσM = ∂x∂x0 g|x0=x (60) for the spectral stresses defined as in Eq. (37). In terms of the Madelung representation [Eqs. (35), (38) and (39)] we get: eσE = n2κ2 2k , eσM = k 2 − k′2 8k3 (61) with the wavenumber k satisfying Eqs. (40) and (41). We also express the quantum Abraham pressure (49) in terms of the Green function. In our one–dimensional case we have ρ ∂ε/∂ρ = χ and so we obtain from Eq. (56) for the spec- tral Abraham pressure: epAb = χκ2g(x, x) = − n2 − 1 n2 eσE . (62) One verifies that from Eqs. (40) and (41) follows the Abraham identity for the quantum stresses (61): ∂x(eσE +eσM) = 2n′ n eσE . (63) Formulas (61) reveal the asymptotics of the stresses for large frequencies (κ → ∞). Equation (42) gives eσE ∼ κn 2 + β0 2κn , eσM ∼ κn 2 − β0 2κn − n′2 8κn3 (64) such that we obtain for the total spectral stress, eσ =eσE +eσM , (65) the asymptotics: eσ ∼ κn − n′2 8κn3 . (66) Like in the three–dimensional planar case27 the asymptotics of the total stress depends only on the local dielectric functions and their first derivatives, in our case on the refractive indexn and its derivative n′ (Fig. 4). We also see that the integrated stresses diverge even in the case of piece–wise homogeneous media when the force density (37) converges. V . RENORMALIZATION Following the scattering approach to Casimir forces36–38 we have developed a theory that directly gives the actual forces on point scatterers, without the need of renormalization. We found that the density of the van der Waals force and the elec- tromagnetic stresses diverge in the macroscopic limit, in direct contradiction to the empirical fact that some colloidal suspen- sions are stable — that liquid paint may remain liquid. Some additional feature must prevent this catastrophic collapse pure van der Waals forces would cause. A. Argument Pitaevskii39 argued that the pressure of the fluid naturally counteracts the van der Waals forces. These counter forces depend on the density and are typically much stronger than the van der Waals forces, such that a small variation in density is sufficient to reach a local mechanical equilibrium. As this small density variation hardly affects the van der Waals force, we ignore it in calculating the force, but we do use the fact8 5 10 κ 20 25 30 0. 0.1 FIG. 4: Asymptotics of the stress. The figure illustrates the asymp- totics of the spectral stress [Eq. (65)] as a function ofκ for the suscep- tibility profile [Eq. (86)] displayed in Fig. 2b (evaluated at x = 0.1). Parameters as in Fig. 2b. The gray curve shows eσ − κn, the renor- malization proposed by Dzyaloshinskii and Pitaevskii 24. One sees that the curve gently decays (as κ−1). The total renormalized stress is given by the integral over κ and diverges. For the black curve the asymptotics (66) is subtracted; the curve decays as κ−3 and this renormalization (Fig. 5) converges. that the counter pressure it causes partly compensates the van der Waals force. This counter pressure appears as the renor- malization of the Casimir force. Which contributions to the van der Waals force are compen- sated? Pitaevskii 39 points out the following. As the counter pressure reacts to density variations, it immediately takes out the part of the quantum force that is also generated by den- sity variations (Sec. IV A): the Abraham pressure [Eq. (49)]. What remains is the divergence of the electromagnetic stress in the material68–70. But, as we argue here, this is not all. The counter pressure can also react to local variations of the elec- tromagnetic stresses, establishing local mechanical equilibria everywhere. We need to define what we mean by local. The electro- magnetic stresses are created by zero–point fluctuations of the field in inhomogeneous media. We may characterize the length scale ℓ of the inhomogeneity by ℓ−1 = |n′/n|. Only frequencies with wavelengths λ ≪ ℓ will contribute to the lo- cal stresses. As λ is inversely proportional to n we obtain the criterion |∇λ| ≪ 1 with λ = 2π κn , (67) which is the well–known condition for the validity of geomet- rical optics or of the semiclassical approximation in quantum mechanics71. We can only define local stresses for fields consistent with geometrical optics. The field at zero frequency will never satisfy this condition, so whenever zero–frequency features are important, the renormalization procedure is bound to fail. This is the case for thermal Casimir forces dominated by the zero Matsubara frequency 72, which might explain why Lif- shitz theory18 does not agree with experiments there41. B. Local stresses Next we assume criterion (67) and work out the local elec- tromagnetic stresses. According to Eq. (60) the stresses are generated by the Green function. We must define a new Green function that captures their local contributions. This g0 we only need in the vicinity of the point of emission x0 and there we assume 27 that it describes a purely outgoing wave unaf- fected by the refractive index profile away from x0. Here is the argument for g0: Green functions must satisfy two conditions: the propa- gation equation (31) and the boundary conditions. The total Green function g is required by the boundary condition (32) to be purely outgoing at ±∞. For g0 we require that it is purely outgoing in an infinitesimal interval around x0 where outgo- ing we define in the sense of geometrical optics. To the left or right of x0 it should propagate like the ψ− or ψ+ waves in the Madelung representation (38) with k = nκ. They obey the condition D±ψ± = 0 with the differential operator D± = ∂x ± κn + n′ 2n . (68) The ∂x ±κn term captures the propagation of the phase, while the n′/(2n) term takes care of the amplitude variation in geo- metrical optics. We thus encode the boundary condition as D±g0 = 0 for x = x0 ± 0 . (69) Being a Green function, g0 must solve the propagation equation (31). For x ̸= x0 it is therefore a solution of the Helmholtz equation (27),i.e. a superposition of the fundamen- tal system ψ± of wave functions: g0 = ( c+−ψ+(x) + c−−ψ−(x) : x < x 0 , c++ψ+(x) + c−+ψ−(x) : x > x 0 . (70) Note that the coefficients depend on x0. At the point of emis- sion, x = x0, the Green function g0 must obey the continu- ity condition (33) and the discontinuity (34) of the derivative. The four conditions of Eqs. (33-34) and (68-69) determine the four coefficients of g0 uniquely. We obtain c±a = ± Daψ∓ 2κn      x0 for a = ± . (71) With these coefficients forg0 we calculate the spectral stresses (60) using the Helmholtz equation (27) for expressing ψ′′ ± as κ2n2ψ±. We obtain the exact result eσE0 = κn 2 , eσM0 = κn 2 + ∂x n′ 4κn2 . (72) We see that eσE0 and eσM0 depend only on the local refrac- tive index n and its first and second derivative. The local Green function g0 does indeed capture local contributions to the electromagnetic stresses. Appendix B compares g0 with the previous renormalizer31 and finds good agreement.9 C. Anomaly We have seen that Eq. (72) describes local stresses, but as Eq. (64) indicates, they do not completely account for the asymptotics of the full quantum electromagnetic stresses at large frequencies ( κ → ∞ ). We use Eq. (43) and write the full asymptotics (64) in terms of the local stresses (72) as eσE ∼eσE0 + β0 2κn , eσM ∼eσM0 + β0 2κn . (73) We see that in both the electric and the magnetic stress there is a characteristic difference by β0/(2κn). For dispersive media β0 will decay sufficiently fast such that the integral of the dif- ference converges, but there is another, deeper problem: the stresses (72) do not satisfy the Abraham identity (63). This is because the full stresses do, and so do the asymptotic stresses (73), but not the terms β0/(2κn). Now, the Abraham identity describes momentum conservation and it is a consequence of Maxwell’s equations. So why is it violated here? In our one–dimensional case, Maxwell’s equations reduce to the Helmholtz equation. One can derive the Abraham iden- tity (63) for the stresses (60) from the Helmholtz equation for the Green function [Eq. (31) at x ̸= x0] and the reciprocity of the Green function: g(x, x0) = g(x0, x) . (74) The reciprocity appears in the structure (35) of the Green function, and it stems from the boundary conditions (32). They state that the field is purely outgoing at infinity. There- fore, the only source of radiation is the emission at point x0. If there are no other sources from infinity we can swap the positions of emitter and receiver and get the same. The local Green function, on the other hand, does satisfy the Helmholtz equation as well, but not the reciprocity, because in order to sustain its boundary conditions (68) and (69) sources at ±∞ are required. Another, direct argument is the following. For g0 the boundary conditions (68-69) depend on the dielectric environment at the point of emission x0. So the emission of radiation depends on n(x0) and n′(x0), but not the reception, which breaks the reciprocity (74). In order to restore momentum conservation [the Abraham identity (63)] we would need to add β0/(2κn) to the spectral stresseseσE0 andeσM0. The extra term is called an anomaly48. Wald48 predicted it first for quantum fields in curved space42. He derived a trace anomaly from the lack of reciprocity of the renormalizer if there is space–time curvature. Inhomoge- neous dielectric media correspond to space–time geometries and vice versa44–47. So it is perhaps not surprising that we ob- tain an anomaly as well. Note however that the van der Waals anomaly is quantitatevely different from Wald’s anomaly48. It was first deduced31 for three–dimensional media with spher- ical symmetry; here we have derived it for the simple one– dimensional case as well. For quantum fields in dielectric media, the anomaly de- scribes the recoil pressure in the material 73. The picture is this: Each infinitesimal cell of the material is emitting and re- ceiving virtual electromagnetic waves described by the Green function g, which creates the electromagnetic stresses. The local contributions to the stresses depend on the local Green function g0, but for g0 emission and reception are no longer interchangeable. The lack of reciprocity creates an imbalance in the recoil of emission and reception that appears as an ad- ditional pressure: the anomaly. Equations (72) derived from the local Green function plus the anomaly capture the divergent contributions to the elec- tromagnetic stresses. As they are local the counter pressure of the suspension should be able to compensate for them. What remains are the effective electromagnetic stresses: eσeff F =eσF −eσF0 − β0 2κn for F = E, M (75) witheσF0 given by Eq. (72) and β0 by Eq. (43). We have thus deduced the renormalizing counter stresses and the anomaly from condition (69). Note that we should introduce a cutoff at low frequencies for being consistent with the criterion (67) for locality. In three dimensions31 such a cutoff would not be nec- essary as the density of states goes withκ2, which sufficiently suppresses the low–frequency contribution. There the crite- rion (67) only matters if the zero frequency becomes singled out and dominant, as in the thermal Casimir effect41. χ σeff -0.4 -0.2 x 0.2 0.4 0. 0.02 0.04 0.06 0 0.5 1 FIG. 5: Renormalized stress. EXAMPLES Let us consider two examples to illustrate our ideas: first a piece–wise homogeneous dielectric of three layers and then a genuinely inhomogeneous medium with sech–squared profile of the susceptibility. The three layers constitute the classic case18 of Casimir forces. We will revisit this case and see in detail how the renormalization in dielectrics emerges there.10 A. V A that counter forces in the material completely compensate this pressure. What is left are the stress differences at the interfaces. From the Green equation (31) follows that g is some superposi- tion of e±nκx in each of the three layers and for x ̸= x0. Reciprocity [Eq. We see from Eq. (60) that these terms cancel each other in the sum (65) of the elec- tromagnetic stresses, whereas the e±nκ(x−x0) terms generate equal electric and magnetic stresses. CONCLUSIONS We have analyzed a simple model for the Casimir force in dielectrics: a chain of point particles interacting with each other by scattering virtual electromagnetic waves. The scatter- ing creates forces4–6 on the particles that appear as the familiar van der Waals forces14 (with retardation taken into account13). The point scatterers may represent the molecules of a dielec- tric medium 59 and they do represent colloidal suspensions 10 such as liquid paint. In our model, we assume both the par- ticles and the field to be confined to one dimension, which has allowed us to develop an efficient numerical algorithm for calculating the forces, and to deduce the macroscopic limit in unprecedented detail and clarity. In the macroscopic limit of infinitely many, infinitely weak scatterers, we found a result that seems both satisfying and paradoxical: the van der Waals force becomes the gradi- ent force of electric field fluctuations, which nicely connects the scattering approach to Casimir forces 36–38 with macro- scopic quantum electrodynamics5,76 and the rich field of opti- cal forces61–65. If the scatterers are arranged to form a piece– wise homogeneous medium the force density is finite, except at interfaces. Yet any inhomogeneity, however minute, will produce infinite forces and bring about the immediate collapse of the suspension. The scatterers appear to anticipate whether they are aligned to form pieces of a homogeneous or of an inhomogeneous medium, which seems paradoxical. It also disagrees with the empirical fact that some colloidal suspen- sions are stable — liquid paint may remain liquid even if it is not perfectly smooth. Homogeneous colloidal suspensions are known to be stable when the long–range attractive van der Waals forces are compensated by other short–range repulsive forces10. We have argued that the same mechanism that makes ho- mogeneous colloidal suspensions stable does also prevent the collapse of inhomogeneous media. The counter forces com- pensate the local contributions to the van der Waals stress, and the remaining non–local contributions constitute the Casimir force. This local balance between the van der Waals and the counter force amounts to the renormalization of the quantum– electromagnetic stresses. We have shown [Eqs. (72) and (75)] that the counter stress depends on the local macroscopic di- electric response functions, the refractive index and its first and second derivative, independent of the microscopic physics involved. Our model describes a suspension of particles in a fluid, but we could also apply it to solids where the inter–atomic forces are even stronger. In either case, the result of the counter pressure is independent of the material details. Only the refractive–index profile counts. Suppose we replace the dielectric material by empty space. Even in this case we need to remove the local stress. For example (Sec.VIA) in each ho- mogenous layer we should remove nκ in the spectral stress [Eq. (83)] — also in empty space where n = 1. Only then the discontinuity of the stress gives the correct Casimir–Lifshitz force17 [Eq. (85)]. One might speculate whether there is some mechanism of counter forces in empty space as well, acting not on the molecular scale of 10−9m, but rather on the Planck scale of 10−35m. The precise microscopic nature of such a space–time medium is unknown, but as in ordinary dielectric materials, the renormalization should not depend on micro- scopic details. In curved space–time, i.e. in gravitational fields 43 the re- lation between space and media is well–known 44–47 in clas- sical electromagnetism: there, Maxwell’s equations are the equations of electromagnetic fields in impedance–matched magneto–electric media45. In our model of quantum electro- magnetism in media, we have reproduced an intriguing fea- ture of renormalization in curved space–time, the van der Waals anomaly (Sec. VC). This dielectric analogue of the trace anomaly48 of quantum fields in curved space42 has been derived before in spherical and planar media 31, here we have deduced it in the most elementary way. In general relativity, the trace anomaly appears in Einstein’s field equations 43 as the cosmological constant73, it has the correct order of magni- tude and is consistent with astronomical data 77, so perhaps it simply is the cosmological constant49,50. Our simple model has come a long way. It has shown to contain rich physics ranging from liquid paint to the cosmo- logical constant. The model is simple enough for elementary calculations, but sufficiently sophisticated for deep cross–field connections. We hope to have made it “as simple as possible, but not simpler”. Appendix A: Numerical procedures Our method of using the transfer matrix for calculating Casimir forces on individual scatterers is efficient and fast, but two problems arise for large wavenumbers κ (becoming rele- vant when the number N of scatterers is large): the products of the exponential propagation matrices (13) in the recurrence relation (14) may cause overflow and the required κ integra- tions in the force formula (9) may be slow or insufficiently ac- curate. We recommend the remedies described here. We also indicate how the calculations can be done in multiple parallel threads. But first we make the following simplification. We do not need the full transfer matrix and its derivative, but only the elements T22 and ∂jT22 for calculating the force [Eq. (9)]. Equations (16) and (25) and the recurrence relations (14) im- ply that bothT22 and ∂jT22 depend only on the second column of A and the second row of B. We may thus replace the A and13 B matrices by N two–dimensional vectors: aj =  A12 A22  , bj =  B21 B22  . (A1) We may give the a and b the initial values a1 = bN =  0 1  (A2) as the eκδ factors in the initial conditions (15) drop out in the ratio T ′ 22/T22. Physically this means that the force is indepen- dent of the propagation to and from the chain of scatterers. 1. Recurrence From the macroscopic limit (Sec. IIIA) we know that the a and b vectors approach the structure (26) and go with so- lutions of the Helmholtz equation (27). Furthermore, from the Madelung representation (38) with asymptotics (42) fol- lows that a and b go as the exponential of R nκ dx, which may grow exponentially large, causing overflow. However, as the force [Eqs. (9) and (12)] depends only on the ratio between T ′ 22 and T22 we may multiply the a and b vectors by some factor, and get the same result. To avoid overflow, we recom- mend to employ the inverse of the macroscopic propagation factor as compensation. This means one should replace the recurrence relations (14) by aj+1 = e−nκδ P Rj aj , bj−1 = e−nκδ P R⊤ j bj with n = p 1 + χj (A3) where the P and Rj denote the matrices P =  e−κδ 0 0 eκδ  , Rj = 1 − κδχj 2  1 1 −1 −1  . (A4) We obtain from Eqs. (16) and (25) for the j–th particle: T22 = bj · Rj aj , ∂ jT22 = −δκ2χj bj · σxaj (A5) in terms of the σx Pauli matrix defined in Eq. (20). Note that for the calculation of the force on one particle the required transfer–matrix elements for all other particles are computed. It is wise to cache them such that they may be used in the force integral for other particles as well. 2. Integration For determining the force on the j–th particle we need to compute the integral Fj = − ℏc 2π Z ∞ 0 ∂jT22 T22 dκ . (A6) For numerical integration, we use a modified variant of the Romberg integration method78 of order 2 × 5. The Romberg method converges much faster than many other numerical in- tegration algorithms, and it also has the advantage that more and more function evaluations in–between the already eval- uated values can be added in each iterative step, until the required precision has been reached. For integrating from zero to infinity, we apply the “Mixed Rule” transformation 78 κ = exp[ν − exp(−ν)] turning Eq. (A6) into an integral from −∞ to +∞ that needs to be cut off. Both cutoff values of ν are determined by an adaptive search algorithm. Further, to make use of the caching–method mentioned above (Sec. A1) we have modified the algorithm such that all possible floating– point values of ν required for the Romberg integration get unique integer values assigned. These values serve as lookup index for the cache. For checking the accuracy of our calcula- tions we used arbitrary–precision arithmetics packages. 3. Parallelization In our software implementation, multiple of the above– described routines are executed in parallel threads. This has to be considered in the caching mechanism, which not only must be thread–safe, but also must be able to deal with the possi- bility that a requested result is in the limbo state of being cur- rently in calculation by another thread. Multi–threading has allowed us to run accurate calculations of the van der Waals force on millions of particles. Appendix B: Previous renormalization How is the renormalization procedure of this paper (Sec. V) related to the previous one 31? In the previous procedure 31, an outgoing Green function is subtracted from the full Green function as well, but this Green function is not given as the ex- act solution of the Green equation (31) with appropriate con- ditions at the source, Eqs. (69) and (68). Rather it comes from the geometrical–optics approximation of the original Green function [Eq. (35)] in the vicinity of the source where, in the renormalizer, n(x) is taken as the quadratic expansion n(x) = n0 + n′ 0(x − x0) + n′′ 0 2 (x − x0)2 (B1) with n0 = n(x0). We have confirmed [Eqs. (43)] and (64)] that the local stresses do only depend on maximally the second derivative of n, which justifies the quadratic expansion (B1). In our case, this is not an assumption, but a consequence of the boundary conditions (69) and (68). The outgoing Green function g0 is represented in terms of the optical length s, the amplitude A and the first-scattering coefficient β1 as g0 = e−κsA  1 + β1s κ  . (B2) The optical length is a solution of the eikonal equation 27 (in one dimension s′2 = n2) as (x − x0) times a term up to14 quadratic order. Integrating the eikonal equation we obtain s = (x − x0)  n0 + n′ 0 2 (x − x0) + n′′ 0 6 (x − x0)2  . (B3) The amplitude satisfies the conservation law 27 (A2s′)′ = 0 (in one dimension). We get by integration: A = − 1 2κ√nn0 (B4) with n given by the quadratic expansion (B1). The integra- tion constant gives the prefactor of the amplitude. It is chosen such that g0 satisfies the Green equation (35) in the immediate vicinity of the delta function where n can be taken as constant n0. The first–scattering coefficient27 β1 obeys 2nβ1 = A′′/A evaluated at x0. We express it in terms of the β0 defined by Eq. (43) and obtain: β1 = β0 n0 . (B5) We substitute these expressions in Eq. (60) for the stresses. Up to κ−1 order we obtain exactly the same result [Eq. (64)] as with the renormalizer advocated in this paper, including the need for the anomaly. The differences lie in the conver- gent part, but it is the convergent part of σ − σ0 that gives the actual physical force. Another difference is conceptual: the new procedure is much more clear and elegant. However, it would be very useful to test the theory in experiments, in- cluding computer experiments, bearing in mind the theorem: for every difficult problem there is a solution that is simple, elegant, and wrong. Appendix C: Three layers In this appendix we consider the case of three homogeneous dielectric layers (as in Sec. VIA) and derive formulas (76-78) for the Green function. Expressions for this Green functions are scattered in the literature, with inconsistent notations, and not all what we need can be found at one place. For the con- venience of the reader we derive them here with an efficient method, the transfer–matrix method (Sec. IIB). Consider the transfer from the one homogenous layer with refractive index n1 to another layer with index n2. In order to satisfy the Helmholtz equation (27) the field ψ and its first derivative ψ′ must be continuous. We thus have in the central layer: ψ+ = en2κx + ϱl e−n2κx , ψ− = e−n2κx + ϱr en2κ(x−2a) . (C5) The Wronskian (30) we read off from Eq. (C5) as W = −2n2κ � 1 − ϱlϱr e−2n2κa . (C6) Expressions (C5) and (C6) give the Green function (35). Note that one can interpret this g(x, x0) as the summed–up geo- metrical series of all multiple reflections 74. In Sec. VIA we need g(x, x) = W −1ψ+(x)ψ−(x) and get Eq. (76) from the expressions derived here. Now turn to the left layer. There ψ+ naturally propagates without scattering as en1κx while ψ− is a superposition of the e±n1κx. Therefore we write ψ+ = en1κx , ψ − = e−n1κx + ϱ en1κx (C7) and have the Wronskian W = −2n1κ . (C8) We require that ψ− ∝ e−n3κx after arriving at the right layer: Tr  e−n2κa 0 0 en2κa  Tl  1 ϱ  ∝  1 0  , (C9) solve for ϱ and express the result in terms of the Fresnel coef- ficients (77): ϱ = − ϱl − ϱre−2n2κa 1 − ϱlϱr e−2n2κa . (C10) Inserting the result (C10) in expressions (C7) for the waves ψ± with Wronskian (C8) we obtain the desired Green function W −1ψ+(x)ψ−(x) and arrive at formula (78). The coefficient (C10) describes the reflection at the entire central layer. The physical interpretation of ϱ becomes appar- ent when we mathematically transform formula (C10) to ϱ = −ϱl + (1 − ϱ2 l ) ϱr e−2n2κa 1 − ϱlϱr e−2n2κa (C11)15 and employ the geometric series 1 1 − ϱlϱr e−2n2κa = ∞X m=0 � ϱlϱr e−2n2κam . (C12) Equations (C11) and (C12) describes the following scenario: a wave comes in from the left. At the left interface a part of the wave is reflected with reflectivity −ϱl (the minus sign because ϱl describes reflection from the right to the left, not from the left to the right). The other part is transmitted with transmittance p 1 − ϱ2 l . The transmitted part propagates to the right interface with factor e−n2κa. If the wave makes it back (such that it counts in the overall reflection coeffi- cient ϱ) it must get reflected at the right interface with coef- ficient ϱr and propagate back to the left interface with factor e−n2κa and be transmitted out of the central layer with trans- mittance p 1 − ϱ2 l . This gives the factor (1 − ϱ2 l ) ϱr e−2n2κa in Eq. (C11). The wave may also get multiply reflected in the central layer, bouncing back and forth multiple times, as the series (C12) describes. , 1 P. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses the unresolved problem of Casimir force/stress inside dielectrics—especially the physical meaning and failure of standard renormalization in inhomogeneous media—by introducing a renormalization-free microscopic model: a 1D chain of point scatterers interacting via vacuum electromagnetic fluctuations. Derives a finite, exact force on each scatterer via scattering theory, then takes the macroscopic limit (infinitely many, infinitely weak scatterers) to obtain a force density f(x)= (ħc/2π)∫₀^∞ κ²χ(x)∂ₓ g(x,x;κ) dκ in terms of the (unrenormalized) Green function. Shows that the macroscopic force density converges for piecewise-homogeneous media (except at interfaces) but diverges for any genuine inhomogeneity, implying an unphysical “instant collapse.” Proposes a physical resolution: short-range restoring/counter forces (pressure) in the medium enforce local mechanical equilibrium and effectively renormalize the Casimir stress; identifies the local counter-stress terms depending only on n, n′, n″ plus an additional ‘anomaly’ term analogous to the trace anomaly in curved spacetime. Recovers standard Lifshitz (Casimir–Lifshitz) forces for three-layer piecewise-homogeneous media as the remaining nonlocal stress after subtracting local contributions.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Models the dielectric as a lattice with spacing δ of point scatterers with polarizabilities α_i, defining a macroscopic susceptibility via χ(x)=α_i/δ and refractive index n=√(1+χ). Computes vacuum (van der Waals/Casimir) forces using the Friedel–Krein–Lloyd density-of-states formula with the scattering phase φ=−i ln det S, then deforms the energy integral to imaginary frequencies to obtain a rapidly convergent force formula Fi=−(ħc/2π)∫₀^∞ ∂_i ln det(1+iK) dκ, relating det(1+iK) to a transfer-matrix element T22. Constructs efficient recurrences for partial transfer matrices/vectors A,B across the chain and expresses ∂_jT in terms of commutators and Pauli matrices, yielding ∂_jT22 in closed form. In the macroscopic limit δ→0, converts recurrences to differential equations equivalent to a 1D Helmholtz equation for modes ψ± and expresses the force density via the Green function g solving (∂ₓ²−κ²n²)g=δ(x−x0). Uses Madelung/WKB-type representation to derive high-κ asymptotics and diagnose divergences. Develops a renormalization interpretation by defining a ‘local’ outgoing Green function g0 via geometrical-optics boundary conditions at the source, extracting local stress contributions σ0 (depending on n,n′,n″) and an additional anomaly term β0/(2κn), leading to effective (renormalized) stresses σ_eff=σ−σ0−anomaly.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: No empirical experiments; validation is theoretical + numerical. Numerical experiments compute forces on finite chains (N≈50–101 in figures; algorithm claimed to scale to millions) for (i) a homogeneous block (piecewise-homogeneous medium) and (ii) an inhomogeneous susceptibility profile χ(x)=χ0 sech²(x/a). Benchmarks/validation include: (1) convergence of discrete-force results to analytic macroscopic-limit formulas (e.g., Eq. 81 for homogeneous block/three-layer case), (2) comparison of spectral force/ stress from discrete transfer-matrix computation to macroscopic Green-function prediction (Eq. 37) and to asymptotics (Eq. 45/66) at large κ, (3) analytic solvable cases: three-layer piecewise-homogeneous Green functions and recovered Lifshitz force (Eq. 85), and closed-form hypergeometric solutions for the sech² profile to compute Green function and Wronskian. Numerical integration over κ uses a modified Romberg method with a “Mixed Rule” variable transform for [0,∞), adaptive cutoffs, overflow-avoidance rescaling in transfer-matrix recurrences, caching, and multithreading.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Model is strictly one-dimensional (field and matter confined to 1D, e.g., waveguide), so quantitative results may not transfer directly to 3D; susceptibility-density relation χ=α/δ is linear as in dilute media, unlike general 3D dense dielectrics. Scatterers are idealized point delta-function susceptibilities; ignores microscopic structure, finite size, and explicit short-range interparticle potentials (introduced only phenomenologically as ‘counter forces’). Divergences appear in macroscopic limit for inhomogeneous profiles; the proposed renormalization relies on assuming local mechanical equilibrium and a locality/geometrical-optics criterion |∇λ|≪1; low-frequency/long-wavelength contributions violate locality, requiring an implicit low-κ cutoff in 1D and potentially failing in thermal Casimir regimes dominated by zero Matsubara frequency. Treatment largely assumes impedance-matched media (μ=1) and focuses on one polarization; dispersion/absorption are only mentioned qualitatively (dispersive α_i allowed) but not fully explored numerically; interfaces still yield singularities in force density (delta-like) even when total forces are finite.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Extend the chain model to higher dimensions or to effectively 3D geometries (planar/spherical) to test universality of the local counter-stress and anomaly terms and connect directly to experimentally relevant setups. Incorporate realistic dispersion and absorption (complex ε(ω)), finite temperature (Matsubara formulation), and explicitly study breakdown of locality at low frequency to assess implications for thermal Casimir experiments. Develop a microscopic model of the counter forces (e.g., explicit short-range repulsion, equation of state of background fluid/solid) and couple density variations self-consistently to electromagnetic stresses to derive renormalization from first principles. Explore dynamics/stability: time-dependent relaxation to local equilibrium and whether instabilities/structure formation occur for inhomogeneous suspensions. Investigate connections to curved-spacetime QFT more systematically: map refractive-index profiles to effective metrics, compute the corresponding trace/anomaly contributions, and test implications for cosmological-constant analogies. Use the efficient algorithm to simulate large-scale heterogeneous media and compare with alternative renormalization prescriptions (virtual work, other stress-tensor schemes) to establish robust, physically justified renormalization rules.
